coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode sensing system for xanthine oxidase detection based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots and iron-cobalt oxide nanosheets
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Junshu
Liu, Jinying
Zhou, Chenyu
Lv, Yuntai
Wang, Nan
Li, Meini
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: As one of the existing forms of xanthine oxidoreductase, xanthine oxidase (XOD) exists in many human tissues, and the overactivity of XOD will result in some serious illnesses such as gout and diabetes. Herein, a novel dual-signal sensing system for XOD activity determination was proposed based on fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and iron-cobalt oxide nanosheets (FeCo-ONSs). XOD has the ability to oxidise xanthine to uric acid while also producing H2O2. As a sort of peroxidase-like enzyme, FeCo-ONSs can catalyze H2O2 to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS). And N, N-Diethyl-p-phenylenediamine sulfate salt (DPD) was oxidized to DPDox that serves as a chromogenic substrate in the presence of ROS, causing the colorless solution to turn pink. DPDox exhibits two characteristic absorption peaks at 551 nm and 512 nm and it can also efficiently quench the fluorescence emission of N-CDs at 500 nm. Therefore, the fluorometric and colorimetric dual-channel sensing platform was established to assay XOD with the lower LOD of 0.00522 mU/mL and 0.0177 mU/mL, respectively. Additionally, the dual-output strategy was found to be applicable to the quantitative analysis of XOD in serum samples with good reliability and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Xanthine oxidase
Peroxidase-like mimetic activity
Dual-signal assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterizations of the N-CDs and FeCo-ONSs
Investigation of peroxidase-mimicking activity of FeCo-ONSs
Feasibility and mechanism of the sensing platform
Fluorometric and colorimetric assay of XOD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N-CDs (the inset is the HRTEM image of the N-CDs) (B) The particle distribution of fluorescent N-CDs (C) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs (D) XPS full spectrum and high-resolution (E) C 1 s, (F) N 1 s, (G) O 1 s XPS spectra of N-CDs (H) XRD patterns of N-CDs (I) Fluorescence excitation, emission and absorption spectra of N-CDs.

(A) SEM image of FeCo-ONSs (B) TEM image of FeCo-ONSs (C) FT-IR spectrum of FeCo-ONSs (D) XPS full spectrum and high-resolution (E) Co 2p, (F) Fe 2p, (G) O 1 s XPS spectra of FeCo-ONSs (H) XRD pattern of FeCo-ONSs (I) UV–vis spectra of DPD, DPD + H2O2, DPD + FeCo-ONSs, and DPD + H2O2 + FeCo-ONSs system.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs + DPD, N-CDs + FeCo-ONSs, N-CDs + xanthine, N-CDs + XOD, N-CDs + DPD + FeCo-ONSs + xanthine + XOD (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs + DPD, N-CDs + FeCo-ONSs, N-CDs + xanthine, N-CDs + XOD, N-CDs + DPD + FeCo-ONSs + xanthine + XOD (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of DPDox and fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs (D) Time-resolved fluorescence decays of N-CDs and N-CDs + DPDox.

(A) Fluorescence response of N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs system after adding different concentrations of H2O2 ranging from 0 to 300 μM(0, 0.4, 6, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 80, 100, 120, 200, 220, 250 and 300 μM) (B) Linear relationship between fluorescent intensity and H2O2 concentration (C) Absorption spectra of N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs system after adding different levels of H2O2 ranging from 0 to 300 μM (0, 2, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 80, 100, 120, 200, 250 and 300 μM) (D) Linear relationship between absorbance intensity and H2O2 concentration.

(A) Fluorescence response of N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs/xanthine system after adding different concentrations of XOD ranging from 0 to 80 mU/mL (0, 0.02, 0.1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 50 and 80 mU/mL) (B) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and XOD concentration (C) Absorption spectra of N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs/xanthine system after adding different levels of XOD ranging from 0 to 80 mU/mL (0, 0.05, 0.08, 0.1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 50 and 80 mU/mL) (D) Linear relationship between absorbance intensity and XOD concentration.
(A, C) Selectivity study of N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs/xanthine system for XOD assay (B, D) Anti-interference capability of N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs/xanthine system for XOD detection.
(A) Graphic outline of synthesizing N-CDs. (B) Schematic diagram of the dual-readout strategy for XOD detection.
Determination of dual-mode methods for XOD activity in serum samples (n=3).

head.ce:title: A dual-mode sensing system for xanthine oxidase detection based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots and iron-cobalt oxide nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one of the existing forms of xanthine oxidoreductase, xanthine oxidase (XOD) exists in many human tissues, and the overactivity of XOD will result in some serious illnesses such as gout and diabetes. Herein, a novel dual-signal sensing system for XOD activity determination was proposed based on fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and iron-cobalt oxide nanosheets (FeCo-ONSs). XOD has the ability to oxidise xanthine to uric acid while also producing H2O2. As a sort of peroxidase-like enzyme, FeCo-ONSs can catalyze H2O2 to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS). And N, N-Diethyl-p-phenylenediamine sulfate salt (DPD) was oxidized to DPDox that serves as a chromogenic substrate in the presence of ROS, causing the colorless solution to turn pink. DPDox exhibits two characteristic absorption peaks at 551 nm and 512 nm and it can also efficiently quench the fluorescence emission of N-CDs at 500 nm. Therefore, the fluorometric and colorimetric dual-channel sensing platform was established to assay XOD with the lower LOD of 0.00522 mU/mL and 0.0177 mU/mL, respectively. Additionally, the dual-output strategy was found to be applicable to the quantitative analysis of XOD in serum samples with good reliability and selectivity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A dual-signal fluorescence and colorimetric sensing system for XOD detection was constructed via fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon dots and iron-cobalt oxide nanosheets with peroxidase-like mimetic properties. XOD can oxidize xanthine into uric acid accompanied by the creation of H2O2. As a sort of peroxidase-like enzyme, FeCo-ONSs have the ability to catalyze H2O2 to produce ROS. In the presence of ROS, the chromogenic substrate DPD could be oxidized to DPDox, making the colorless solution turn pink. DPDox has two characteristic absorption peaks at 551 nm and 512 nm and is capable of quenching the fluorescence emission of N-CDs at 500 nm. Therefore, the fluorometric and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platform could be applied to assay XOD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Xanthine oxidase (XOD) is a metal flavoprotein containing molybdenum, non-heme iron and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) [1]. XOD catalyzes xanthine into uric acid whose excessive presence in the blood may result in the occurrence of diseases such as hyperuricemia and gout [2,3]. Additionally, the reaction is also accompanied by the production of hydrogen peroxide, reactive oxygen and nitrogen species [4,5], which can induce the development of acute ischemic stroke [6], Duchenne muscular dystrophy [7], hemolytic diseases [8] and a sizable proportion of all cancers [9,10]. As a result, supervising XOD concentration in blood is a crucial adjective for clinical studies. Over the years, a variety of methods for XOD detection have been reported, including fluorescence method [11], colorimetric method [12], phosphorescent method [13], photoelectrochemical method [14], etc. Chang et al. constructed a BTB-MOF based fluorescence probe for monitoring XOD, and its fluorescence emission response to the substrate and oxidative product was different [15]. Wang et al. proposed a colorimetric sensing system for the quantitative assay of XOD based on the Fe3O4@MWCNTs/Hemin nanocomposite with peroxidase-like mimetic activity [16]. Nevertheless, some potential interferences in biological samples may affect the single colorimetric or fluorescent signal output, making the detection results inaccurate. Hence, developing a sensitive and accurate XOD detection method with more than one signal output is of great concern.

Nanozymes have generated significant interest owing to their particular superiority, such as controllable catalytic ability, cost-effectiveness, ease of synthesis and storage [17–19]. For the past few years, many kinds of nanozymes containing carbon-based nanozymes [20], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)-based nanozymes [21], metal oxide-based nanozymes [22] and metal sulfide-based nanozymes [23], have been applied in biosensing [24], antitumor therapy [25], environmental monitoring [26] and food contaminants detection [27]. As typical bimetallic oxide nanozymes, iron-cobalt oxide nanosheets (FeCo-ONSs) have been explored as electrode materials for supercapacitors[28] or antibiotics elimination [29]. Nevertheless, the application of the FeCo-ONSs in bio-enzyme detection has rarely been reported.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been explored extensively because of their wide range of raw materials, simple synthesis method, excellent optical properties, low cost, nontoxicity, and good biocompatibility [30,31], which make CDs widely used in vivo bioimaging and theranostics [32], electrocatalysis [33], fluorescent probes [34], antibacterial [35], bone defect repair [36], environmental treatment [37], biosensing [38] and other fields. In recent years, doped CDs have aroused broad concern on account of their higher quantum yield compared with pure CDs [39]. Nitrogen is the most commonly used doping element of CDs due to its comparable size with carbon and multifarious N-based chemical structures [40,41].

Herein, N-doped CDs and FeCo-ONSs were selected to construct a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing method to detect XOD activity. As displayed in Scheme 1A, N-CDs were prepared at room temperature by continuously stirring o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and glyoxal (40 %). With the aid of XOD, xanthine can be converted to uric acid in the presence of oxygen (O2) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is generated simultaneously (Fig. S1). The product H2O2 can be effectively catalyzed to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) by bimetallic oxidase nanozyme FeCo-ONSs with peroxidase-like mimetic properties, and then colorless N, N-Diethyl-p-phenylenediamine sulfate (DPD) could be oxidized into pink oxidized DPD (DPDox) through ROS, which exhibits characteristic absorption peaks at 551 nm and 512 nm. This can lead to the quenching of the fluorescence emission of N-CDs at 500 nm. The decreasing emission degrees of N-CDs and increasing absorption intensities of DPDox could be employed for the quantitative assay of XOD activity (Scheme 1B). The established sensing platform outputs two signals, making the result more accurate.
Chemicals and apparatus were provided in the supplementary information.

The fluorescent N-CDs were prepared at room temperature according to the literature with a minor modification [42]. Initially, 0.2 g OPD was added to 8 mL ultrapure water under ultrasound to ensure complete dissolution. Then, glyoxal (40 %, 48 mL) was mixed with OPD solution (0.025 g/mL, 8 mL) in a beaker with continuous stirring under ambient temperature for 3 h. Afterwards, NaOH (1 M) was added to the mixture until no more yellow precipitates were generated (pH=11). The N-CDs product was obtained by centrifuging the above mixture at 10,000 rpm to move impurities and then repeatedly rinsing with water. Finally, the preparation was dissolved by 0.1 M HCl and stored at 4 ℃ for subsequent use. The quantum yield of N-CDs was calculated to be 6.07 %, which determined by using quinine sulfate as a fluorescence standard.
The FeCo-ONSs were prepared based on a previous report with some slight modifications [43]. The detailed preparation procedure was provided in the supplementary information.

A series of different levels of XOD solutions were mixed with 100 μL xanthine (300 μM) in 10 μL Tris−HCl buffer solution (pH 8.1, 100 mM), and incubated at 37 ℃ for 60 min. Then, 100 μL of HAc−NaAc buffer solution (pH 4.5, 100 mM), 40 μL of DPD (10 mM), 60 μL of FeCo-ONSs (200 μg/mL), and 85 μL of N-CDs were mixed with the above solution. The fluorescence emission spectra (excited at 380 nm) and the UV−vis absorption spectra were obtained after reacting at 20℃ for 30 min.

Human serum samples provided by hospital (Changchun China-Japan Union Hospital) were diluted with ultrapure water for follow-up experiments. Three different concentrations of XOD were added into the prepared samples, and measurement procedures were the same as those described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the N-CDs and FeCo-ONSs
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in Fig. 1A, the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image showed the synthesized N-CDs were spherical shapes, exhibited good dispersion and had a particle size distribution around 6.43 nm ( Fig. 1B). As shown in the inset of Fig. 1A, the HRTEM image demonstrated the N-CDs exhibited a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, manifesting their graphite characteristics. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT–IR) was applied to study the surface structures of N-CDs (Fig. 1C). The intense peak at 3391 cm−1 was corresponded to O−H and N−H bonds. The characteristic bands that appeared at 1449 cm−1, 1083 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 were matched to −COO– stretching vibration, stretching and bending vibrations of C−O and CN bonds, respectively. As mentioned above, the amino group in the raw material underwent an amine-aldol condensation reaction with the aldehyde group to form the Schiff base, which eventually obtained the N-CDs. X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS) in Fig. 1D revealed the elemental composition of the prepared N-CDs was C (285.0 eV), N (398.7 eV) and O (531.4 eV). In the C1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 1E), four peaks were observed at 284.4 eV, 285.0 eV, 285.8 eV and 286.5 eV, C−C/CC, C sp3 from C−O, CC−N and CO/CN, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) displayed four peaks at 401.2 eV, 400.4 eV, 399.5 eV and 398.7 eV, which matched to N−H, N−(C)3, C−N−C and CN. Furthermore, as Fig. 1G displayed, three peaks of O1s spectrum was observed at 532.8 eV, 532.0 eV and 531.2 eV, which could correspond to OC−O*, *OC−O and −OH, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. 1H had an evident peak at around 2θ = 25.2◦, corresponding to the diffraction of the (002) plane of carbon crystal [44]. The EDS spectrum of N-CDs also confirmed the presence of C, N and O element in the N-CDs (Fig. S2), the presence of elemental Cl was due to the N-CDs powder finally dissolving in 0.1 M HCl. The optical properties of the N-CDs were also explored. As Fig. 1I displayed, the absorption spectrum exhibited two apparent peaks at 248 nm and 275 nm, which were attributed to π-π* transitions, and a weak peak at around 357 nm, which was ascribed to the n-π* transition from CO [45,46]. In addition, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra showed the maximal emission wavelength of N-CDs was 504 nm under the excitation of 381 nm. The inset of Fig. 1I exhibited that the N-CDs solution was brown under sunlight and emitted yellow-green fluorescence under ultraviolet lamp irradiation. Furthermore, N-CDs showed an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission with the excitation wavelengths changed from 360 nm to 500 nm (Fig. S3A). From Fig. S3B, N-CDs had stronger fluorescence in acidic medium and then gradually declined when pH varied from 3.0 to 8.0. Moreover, the photostability of the N-CDs was explored. As exhibited in Fig. S3C, the fluorescence of N-CDs kept stable under the irradiation of xenon lamp for 1 h, which manifested that N-CDs had excellent resistance to photobleaching. From Fig. S3D, N-CDs showed stable fluorescence intensity as the concentration of NaCl changed from 0 to 100 mM, manifesting that N-CDs exhibit excellent salt stability. As aforementioned, N-CDs had remarkable fluorescence properties.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) image illustrated the FeCo-ONSs were composed of interrelated frizzy nanosheets with ruffled morphology which had plentiful wrinkles ( Fig. 2A). Additionally, TEM analysis revealed FeCo-ONSs were distinctly two-dimensional lamellar structures (Fig. 2B). Moreover, FT-IR analysis in Fig. 2C was implemented to evaluate the surface structure of FeCo-ONSs. The intense peaks observed at 3399 cm−1 and 1624 cm−1 were severally corresponded to the stretching and bending vibration of the O−H group present in the water molecules on the surface of the FeCo-ONSs. The 2939 cm−1 and 1098 cm−1 peaks were derived from the stretching vibration of CO and −CH2, respectively. Furthermore, the 482 cm−1 and 587 cm−1 peaks were attributed to the Co−O and Fe−O stretching vibration, respectively. Finally, the 1383 cm−1 peak was derived from the stretching vibration of NO3 −, indicating the presence of remanent NO3 − on the surface of FeCo-ONSs. As illustrated in Fig. 2D, the XPS full spectrum of FeCo-ONSs revealed it contained cobalt, iron and oxygen elements. Fig. 2E showed three characteristic Co 2p3/2 binding energies. The peaks appeared at 781.1 eV and 782.9 eV were derived from Co (II) and Co (III) in the octahedral sites, respectively. The 782.1 eV peak was derived from Co (II) in the tetrahedral sites. The characteristic Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 peaks were appeared at 712.9 eV and 724.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 2F). The peaks observed at 710.8 eV and 712.2 eV were corresponded to Fe (II) and Fe (III), respectively. As Fig. 2G displayed, the O1s peaks located at 530.4 eV, 531.7 eV and 532.1 eV were attributed to M−O、M−OH and H−O−H bands, respectively. XRD pattern in Fig. 2H was employed to study the crystalline structures of the obtained FeCo-ONSs. There were two peaks at 35.1° and 62.5° corresponded to the (311) and (440) planes, compared to the standard structure of CoFe2O4 (JCPDS No. 22−1086), indicating the prepared FeCo-ONSs had a low crystallinity structure [47]. All above-mentioned clarified the successful synthesis of FeCo-ONSs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of peroxidase-mimicking activity of FeCo-ONSs
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the catalytical performance of FeCo-ONSs, DPD was chosen as the peroxidase substrate. As Fig. 2I illustrated, DPD could be oxidized by FeCo-ONSs with the presence of H2O2, and the oxidation products had two characteristic absorption peaks at 551 nm and 512 nm. On the contrary, when only H2O2 or FeCo-ONSs were present, there was nearly no absorption peak appeared. Therefore, FeCo-ONSs possess peroxidase-like mimetic activity.

For the purpose of optimizing reaction conditions, we investigated the influences of reaction pH, temperature and time on DPD/FeCo-ONSs/H2O2 system. Fig. S4A showed that the absorbance intensity changed with the pH value varied from3.0 to 5.7, and the best value emerged at pH 4.5. Fig. S4B revealed that the optimal reaction temperature was 20 ℃. Finally, as revealed in Fig. S4C, the absorbance intensity continued to increase with the extension of reaction duration and then almost remained stable after 30 min. Thus, pH values of 4.5, 20 ℃ and 30 min were selected in the further assay.

To explore the peroxidase-mimicking activity of FeCo-ONSs, we investigated the catalytic mechanism and kinetic parameters using DPD as the colorimetric substrate under the optimal conditions. Firstly, reactive oxygen species were differentiated using p-benzoquinone (pBQ), isopropanol (IPA) and sodium azide (NaN3) to capture superoxide radical (O2 •−), hydroxyl radical (•OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2), respectively [48,49]. As Fig. S5A displayed, no absorption variation was observed when pBQ or NaN3 was added to FeCo-ONSs/DPD/ H2O2 system. On the contrary, when IPA was added to the system, the absorption decreased distinctly. Furthermore, with the increase in the concentration of IPA, the absorption decreased more obviously. The above results demonstrated that the active intermediate was •OH in FeCo-ONSs/H2O2/DPD system. Moreover, terephthalic acid (TA) was selected to affirm the production of •OH [50]. As shown in Fig. S5B, the fluorescence emission at 426 nm gradually increased with the increase of FeCo-ONSs in the H2O2/FeCo-ONSs system, which further indicating the generation of •OH. Therefore, the catalytic mechanism of FeCo-ONSs/H2O2/DPD system was FeCo-ONSs catalyze H2O2 to produce •OH which further oxidized DPD to DPDox. Moreover, the Michaelis-Menten curves and Lineweaver-Burk plots were exhibited in Fig. S6. The Km value of FeCo-ONSs towards DPD and H2O2 is lower compared with horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and some previously reported mimetic peroxidases (Table S1). This high affinity with H2O2 and DPD indicated that FeCo-ONSs was an excellent nanozyme with peroxidase-like mimetic properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and mechanism of the sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility and sensing mechanism of the N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs/xanthine sensing system for XOD activity detection were investigated by studying the fluorescence and absorbance intensity changes when N-CDs were incubated with different substrates. As demonstrated in Fig. 3A, N-CDs showed strong fluorescence emission when DPD, FeCo-ONSs, xanthine or XOD existed in this system separately, demonstrating that DPD, FeCo-ONSs, xanthine and XOD alone do not affect the fluorescence emission of N-CDs. In contrast, when N-CDs, DPD, FeCo-ONSs, xanthine and XOD coexisted in the solution, the emission of the N-CDs decreased distinctly which resulted from the production of DPDox. As a result, the absorption peaks at 551 nm and 512 nm could be observed. However, no absorption peak was observed when DPD, FeCo-ONSs, xanthine or XOD existed in this system alone (Fig. 3B). The above results confirmed the feasibility of the N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs/xanthine dual-mode sensing system for XOD assay. The possible mechanism of the as-proposed sensing system was then explored (Scheme 1). XOD can oxidize xanthine with oxygen and then generate H2O2. FeCo-ONSs could oxidize DPD to DPDox in the presence of H2O2 due to its peroxidase-mimicking activity. Moreover, DPDox exhibits characteristic absorption peaks at 551 nm and 512 nm, which can cause the quenching of the fluorescence emission of N-CDs at 500 nm. Fig. 3C illustrated that the emission and excitation bands of N-CDs partially overlapped with the absorption curve of DPDox. Furthermore, the fluorescence decay curve of N-CDs and N-CDs/DPDox were detected. The fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs was 2.93 ns, while N-CDs/ DPDox was 2.50 ns (Fig. 3D). The fluorescence lifetime showed a decrease when DPDox was coexisted with N-CDs, indicating the quenching mechanism was fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET).

To achieve good analytical performance of the established dual-signal assay method for XOD, some relevant factors were optimized. Fig. S7A revealed that when pH value increased in the range of 6.0–9.3, the absorbance intensity achieved its maximum at pH 8.1. Thus pH 8.1 was chosen for the experimental conditions. Furthermore, the incubation temperature and reaction time between xanthine and XOD were also optimized. Fig. S7B demonstrated that the absorbance intensity reached the maximum at 37 ℃. As exhibited in Fig. S7C that the absorbance intensity was virtually inclined to level off after incubation of 60 min. Hence, 60 min was chosen for the experimental incubation time. Fig. S7D revealed that the absorbance intensity attained a maximum at 300 μM xanthine. Thus, 37 ℃, 60 min and 300 μM of the xanthine were selected for the sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric and colorimetric assay of XOD
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum parameters, the analytical performance of the N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs system for H2O2 detection was first investigated. Fig. 4A displayed that the fluorescence intensity of the assay system declined with improving H2O2 concentration, and displayed a linear relationship from 0.4 μM to 220 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.113 μM (Fig. 4B). The linear equation was F = 3557.3 – 11.77 [H2O2] (μM, R2=0.997). Similarly, as Fig. 4C exhibited, the absorbance intensity at 551 nm increased gradually with improving H2O2 concentration, and had a favorable linear relationship with H2O2 concentration increased from 2 μM to 250 μM (Fig. 4D). The correlation equation was A = 0.0019 [H2O2] + 0.2190 (μM, R2 = 0.997), and the LOD was 0.656 μM. Thus, the N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs/xanthine-based system can be applied for H2O2 detection, which could be further used to detect XOD.

Under the optimum parameters, the fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform for XOD examination was further inspected. As exhibited in Fig. 5A, when XOD concentration increased in the 0–80 mU/mL range, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs/xanthine system continuously decreased. The linear equation was F = 2839.4 − 165.18 [XOD] (mU/mL, R2=0.997), which ranged from 0.02 mU/mL to 8 mU/mL, and had a low LOD of 0.00522 mU/mL (Fig. 5B). Meanwhile, the absorbance spectra of the N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs/xanthine system with various concentrations of XOD were explored. As Fig. 5C displayed, the absorbance intensity at 551 nm elevated when the XOD concentration increased from 0 to 80 mU/mL, and showed an excellent linear relationship from 0.05 mU/mL to 10 mU/mL, with the LOD of 0.0177 mU/mL (Fig. 5D). The corresponding regression equation was A = 0.0486 [XOD] + 0.2619 (mU/mL, R2 = 0.995). The reproducibility of the method has been explored with a concentration of 8 mU/mL XOD. The RSDs of the fluorometric and colorimetric assay were 1.68 % and 0.75 %, respectively. Compared to most previous reports, the constructed sensing system has a lower LOD, suggesting that it is more sensitive for monitoring XOD activity (Table S2).

To assess the practicability of the N-CDs/DPD/FeCo-ONSs/xanthine sensing system for XOD assay, we conducted selectivity and anti-interference examination by adding a series of potential interfering substances: inorganic ions (Fe3+, Ca2+, NO3 −, Cl−, K+, Na+, Mg2+, SO4 2-), enzymes (Papain, Trypsin, Glucose oxidase, ascorbic acid oxidase, sarcosine oxidase, lysozyme and α-glucosidase), biomolecules (Galactose, Urea, Sucrose, Glucose, fructose, lactose, maltose) and amino acids (Glutamate, Threonine, Alanine, Arginine, Glycine). As shown in Fig. 6A and C, this sensing system had obvious changes in the presence of XOD while others could not exhibit evident influence on the fluorometric and colorimetric values. Furthermore, the existence of interfering substances was almost unable to change the fluorescence signal and absorption intensity response to XOD (Fig. 6B and D). The above-mentioned illustrated that the assay platform had great sensitivity and anti-interference ability.

To verify the practical applicability of the established method, normal human and hyperuricemia patient serum samples were spiked with three concentrations of XOD for analysis. Table 1 showed the recoveries of the fluorometric assay were in the range of 99.71 %−104.04 %, with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) no more than 4.0 %. Similarly, the recoveries of the colorimetric assay were in the range of 99.75 %−104.34 %, with the RSDs less than 4.7 %. All of the above indicated the dual-mode strategy had potential application prospect in accessing XOD activity in practical samples.

In summary, an innovative dual-signal fluorometric and colorimetric analytical strategy was established for XOD activity determination with nitrogen-doped carbon dots and iron-cobalt oxide nanosheets. The fluorescence and colorimetric methods showed wider linear ranges and lower LODs of 0.00522 mU/mL and 0.0177 mU/mL, respectively. In contrast to single-signal sensing strategies, the proposed method is more reliable with excellent selectivity and anti-interference. This dual-mode sensing platform showed good results for XOD activity monitoring in practical samples and has promising potential in biological analytical fields.

Xingguang Su: Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Meini Li: Formal analysis. Nan Wang: Data curation. Yuntai Lv: Software. Chenyu Zhou: Investigation. Jinying Liu: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Junshu Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper:

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22374058), the Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin Province, China (No.20220402001GH), the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, China (No. SKLEAC202201).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135954.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel reversible inhibitors of xanthine oxidase targeting the active site of the enzyme

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Junshu Wang is currently carrying out her Master's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the synthesis of quantum dots and their applications for biosensors.

Jinying Liu is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the synthesis of quantum dots and their applications for biosensors.

Chenyu Zhou is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is about the synthesis of nanozymes and their application on sensing.

Yuntai Lv is currently carrying out his Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). His research is about the synthesis of nanozymes and their application on sensing.

Nan Wang is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is about the synthesis of nanozymes and their application for biosensors.

Meini Li is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is about the synthesis of nanozymes and their application on sensing.

Xingguang Su is a professor at the Department of Analytical Chemistry at the College of Chemistry, Jilin University. She received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 1992 and her Ph.D. degree from Jilin University (China) in 1999. Her research focuses on the synthesis, characterization, functionalization and application of quantum dots and quantum dots-tagged microspheres in biomedicine.
